EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Forcing associated with a system over northern Minnesota/Wisconsin helped generate a round of severe thunderstorms ahead of an attendant cold front moving across the Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening of 4/24. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blown down over powerlines near Billings Twp. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration powerful fall storm impacted much of west and north central Upper Michigan from the 16th through the 18th with damaging winds, heavy wet snow, and some lakeshore erosion and flooding near Lake Superior.  The storm and its impacts attracted national and regional media attention with the NWS Marquette staff giving numerous phone and video interviews leading up to and during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow amounts generally ranged from one to three inches during the event with the  greatest total of 3.8 inches measured at Painesdale.  North winds gusted to near 40 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration powerful fall storm impacted much of west and north central Upper Michigan from the 16th through the 18th with damaging winds, heavy wet snow, and some lakeshore erosion and flooding near Lake Superior.  The storm and its impacts attracted national and regional media attention with the NWS Marquette staff giving numerous phone and video interviews leading up to and during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Copper Harbor ASOS measured north wind gusts in excess of 45 mph throughout much of the period with a peak gust of 56 mph around 2 am on the 18th. Some minor tree damage occurred with the higher winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration powerful fall storm impacted much of west and north central Upper Michigan from the 16th through the 18th with damaging winds, heavy wet snow, and some lakeshore erosion and flooding near Lake Superior.  The storm and its impacts attracted national and regional media attention with the NWS Marquette staff giving numerous phone and video interviews leading up to and during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snowfall totals over northern Iron County were estimated to be 4 to 7 inches during the event with locally higher amounts near 10 inches.  The wet snow combined with north winds gusting near 40 mph at times caused some tree damage and knocked power out to nearly 600 customers. One to locally four inch snow amounts were common over the southern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime instability showers produced heavy accumulation of pea-sized hail or graupel over eastern portions of Marquette County on the afternoon of the 13th.  At least one automobile accident was attributed to the heavy accumulation of graupel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of heavy accumulation of pea-sized hail or graupel in the Harvey area during the late afternoon of the 13th.  The spotter at Green Garden measured graupel accumulation of 2.5 inches in 45 minutes.  The heavy graupel  accumulation caused hazardous driving conditions along US-41 south of Harvey, and at least one car crash was reported by the Marquette County Central Dispatch.||Significant graupel accumulation was also observed near Gwinn during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration powerful fall storm impacted much of west and north central Upper Michigan from the 16th through the 18th with damaging winds, heavy wet snow, and some lakeshore erosion and flooding near Lake Superior.  The storm and its impacts attracted national and regional media attention with the NWS Marquette staff giving numerous phone and video interviews leading up to and during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports included 12-14 inches of wet snow in the Ironwood area and over 12 inches in Wakefield.  The heavy wet snow combined with north winds gusting near 30 mph caused power outages to more than 1000 customers during the event and  closed or delayed many Gogebic County schools on the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying shortwave traversed the southern Great Lakes/Ohio Valley region from 1/5 into 1/6, providing favorable ascent aloft to support a sub-1000mb clipper. This system trekked across northern Michigan, bringing an enhanced band of snow to most of the area during the afternoon of 1/5. Lake effect snow continued behind the clipper into the morning of 1/6, producing snowfall amounts of 7-10+ across areas of northern lower MI near Lake Michigan south of Grand Traverse Bay as well as the Rogers City area in Presque Isle county. The highest totals of 10.2 and 10 were measured in Leelanau and Benzie counties respectively with another 9 report coming from Manistee county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed across southwest Lower Michigan Thursday afternoon producing some additional light snow accumulations and some hazardous travel.  An intense single band dropped into Berrien and Cass Counties Thursday evening into the early morning hours of Friday with 1-2 snowfall rates per hour.  Numerous slide-offs were reported and many area schools were closed on Friday January 7.  Total snow accumulations from Thursday afternoon into Friday morning ranged from 8 to 16 inches across Berrien and Cass Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed on the afternoon of Thursday January 6th, but a more intense single band of heavy lake effect snow affected Berrien County Thursday evening into the early overnight Friday morning.  Snowfall rates of 2 inches per hour accompanied the heavier lake effect snow band with numerous reports of slideoffs and whiteout conditions. Numerous schools were closed on Friday.  Snowfall amounts from Thursday afternoon into early Friday morning ranged from 8 to 16 inches.  The greatest snowfall occurred across the northern and northeast portions of the county.  A storm total of 19 inches was reported from Eau Claire, MI, but this also included some lake effect snow that occurred Wednesday into early Thursday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed across southwest Lower Michigan Thursday afternoon producing some additional light snow accumulations and some hazardous travel.  An intense single band dropped into Berrien and Cass Counties Thursday evening into the early morning hours of Friday with 1-2 snowfall rates per hour.  Numerous slide-offs were reported and many area schools were closed on Friday January 7.  Total snow accumulations from Thursday afternoon into Friday morning ranged from 8 to 16 inches across Berrien and Cass Counties.

Snowfall amounts from Thursday afternoon into early Friday morning ranged from 8 to 16 inches.  The highest amounts were reported across the western portions of the county.  A storm total of 15 inches was reported from 1 mile south-southeast of Indian Lake, MI, but this also included some lake effect snow that occurred Wednesday into early Thursday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed across southwest Lower Michigan the afternoon of January 19th producing some light snow accumulations. An intense single band dropped into Berrien County during the late evening on January 19th.  The band was progressive through most of the county, but persisted the longest across far southwest portions of the county where storm total snowfall approached 6 inches. This created hazardous travel conditions with reports of slideoffs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed on the afternoon of January 19th, but a more intense single band of heavy lake effect snow affected moved into the county late in the evening on January 19th. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour accompanied the heavier lake effect snow band with numerous reports of slideoffs. Snowfall amounts ranged from around 1 inch across eastern portions of the county to 4 to 6 inches across the west and southwest portions of the county. A storm total of 6.0 inches was reported from 1 mile NNE of Three Oaks, MI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration powerful fall storm impacted much of west and north central Upper Michigan from the 16th through the 18th with damaging winds, heavy wet snow, and some lakeshore erosion and flooding near Lake Superior.  The storm and its impacts attracted national and regional media attention with the NWS Marquette staff giving numerous phone and video interviews leading up to and during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two-day storm total accumulation of wet snowfall around Marquette County included between 13 and 18 inches near National Mine,  14 inches at Clarkburg,  13 inches at the National Weather Service in Negaunee Township and around 10 inches from Sands Township to near Little Lake.  The heavy wet snow combined with north wind gusts between 45 and 60 mph to cause extensive tree damage and widespread power outages to nearly 15,000 customers.  Schools were closed throughout the county on the 18th.  ||Storm force north winds on Lake Superior whipped up waves as high as 20 feet near the Lake Superior shoreline.  The pounding surf and threat of beach erosion caused Marquette city officials to close Lakeshore Boulevard from Picnic Rocks to Presque Isle Park from the 17th into the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a period of moderate to heavy snow during the afternoon and evening hours of February 17th. Total snow accumulations ranged between 3 and 7 inches, with some light ice and sleet accumulations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a brief period of sleet and freezing rain, and then over to all snow during the afternoon hours of February 17th. The snow, moderate to heavy at times, continued into the evening hours. Heavy snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour reduced visibilities to less than a quarter of a mile at times which led to difficult travel conditions. Snowfall totals across the county generally ranged between 4 and 7 inches. Light ice and sleet accumulations were also achieved before the switch over to all snow occurred. There were reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive longwave trough pivoted across the Great Lakes 1/9 into 1/10 and worked to deepen a cyclone crossing Hudson Bay. Subsidence behind the wave helped build strong high pressure across much of the central CONUS. A tightened pressure gradient across the Great Lakes in between these two features resulted in enhanced WNW low-level flow and associated lake effect snow across the region. Cold temperatures lead to small flakes across most of the region, but enhanced convergence with lake aggregate troughing and land breeze formation from the eastern upper peninsula and Ontario, Canada resulted in heavy lake effect snow across parts of Chippewa county where an official event-total snowfall amount of 16.1 was measured in Sault Ste Marie. In 24 hours from 7am EST 1/9 to 7am EST 1/10, 14.2 was measured at the site. Cold temperatures and gusty winds also resulted in significant visibility impacts, closing down a large portion of US-2 during the afternoon of 1/9 due to whiteout conditions.

Schools were closed throughout Baraga County on the 22nd and were on a two-hour delay on the 23rd due to impacts from the heavy snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers over higher terrain locations of Baraga County received anywhere from 7 to 10 inches of lake effect snow over a 24-hour period.  The heavy snow combined with northwest winds gusting near 40 mph caused considerable blowing and drifting of snow and near whiteout conditions at times during the early morning of the 19th.

The storm also packing northwest wind gusts over 60 mph brought blizzard conditions and power outages to Alger County during the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer in Steuben measured 6.3 inches of lake effect snow in less than 24 hours.  The snow combined with gusty northwest winds near 40 mph at times caused occasional whiteout conditions in blowing snow during the early morning hours of the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave progressed over the Great Lakes 2/18 into 2/19, providing favorable ascent aloft to support a deepening sub-1000mb cyclone that trekked across the upper peninsula of Michigan into Ontario. This system brought heavy snowfall in a short period of time, along with wind gusts in excess of 50 mph at times that lead to significant drops in visibility. Western Mackinac county saw the highest snowfall totals of 10-12 with a widespread 6-9 reported across northwest lower Michigan.

A strong winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow and considerable blowing snow to portions of west and north central Upper Michigan from the 18th into the 19th. The storm also packing northwest wind gusts over 60 mph brought blizzard conditions and power outages to Alger County during the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly ten inches of lake effect snow accumulation in Grand Marais combined with strong northwest winds gusting to near 60 mph resulted in blizzard conditions on the morning of the 19th.  The blizzard conditions were very challenging for mushers in the annual UP200 dog sled race.  Five out of 12 teams that started the race were scratched.  A large tree downed by the strong winds caused further delays when it had to be removed from the race trail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 6 to 9 inches of lake effect snowfall over less than a 24-hour period combined with northwest winds gusting 40 to 50 mph at times resulted in blizzard conditions across northern Schoolcraft County during the early morning hours of the 19th.

A large storm system impacted the region, with rain turning over to all snow on Wednesday February 2nd.  The transition to snow took longest across Metro Detroit and points south, which resulted in only 2-4 inches of accumulation. The heaviest snow band set up along and north of the I-69/Flint vicinity, where up to a foot of snow was reported. Here are some of the higher totals received:||Frankenmuth (5 SE)... 12 inches.|Birch Run (2 Southeast)... 11.2 inches.|Columbiaville (2 NW)... 11 inches.|Flint Bishop Airport... 11 inches. |Owosso... 9 to 11 inches.|Juhl (2E)... 9 inches.|Howell... 8 inches.|Chesaning... 8 inches.

A winter storm brought 4-6 inches of snowfall along and south of I-69 with locally higher amounts up to 7 inches near metro Detroit. The initial round of wintry mix produced slick conditions for untreated surfaces (i.e. sidewalks and parking lots), while snow covered roads during the evening created hazardous travel conditions for drivers. Additionally, strong northerly winds behind the front produced some areas of blowing snow late Thursday evening until high pressure worked in early Friday morning. Here are some of the higher totals received:||Grosse Pointe (4N)... 7 inches.|Roseville... 6.8 inches.|Wyandotte... 6.8 inches.|Eastpointe.... 6 inches.|Ann Arbor... 5.5 inches.|Linden.... 5 inches.|Rochester.... 5 inches.

A low pressure system and its associated cold front tracking across the Upper Great Lakes brought moderate lake effect snow to portions of western Upper Michigan from the 9th into the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter at Twin Lakes measured seven inches of lake effect snow in 24 hours.  Observers in the Houghton/Hancock area measured closer to five inches in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Black river near Jedo in northern St. Clair county exceeded it banks on February 17th, which lead to some flooded roads. The river peaked just under 10 and half feet.

The storm also packing northwest wind gusts over 60 mph brought blizzard conditions and power outages to Alger County during the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers in Painesdale and Twin Lakes measured nearly nine inches of lake effect snow in 24 hours.  The snow combined with northwest winds gusting as high as 45 mph resulted in occasional whiteout conditions in blowing snow at times across northern Houghton County.

The storm also packing northwest wind gusts over 60 mph brought blizzard conditions and power outages to Alger County during the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated four to five inches of lake effect snow fell over a 24-hour period.   Northwest winds gusting as high as 50 to 60 mph also caused near whiteout conditions at times in blowing snow.

The storm also packing northwest wind gusts over 60 mph brought blizzard conditions and power outages to Alger County during the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter from Big Bay Point measured a northwest wind gust of 64 mph shortly after midnight on the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of upper disturbances lifting up from the Central Plains produced periods of moderate snow over portions of Upper Michigan while sustaining a long-duration northeast flow lake enhanced snow event into north central Upper Michigan from the 21st into the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Keweenaw County Road Commission measured a two-day lake effect snowfall total of 26.5 inches near Phoenix. East winds gusting near 35 mph also aused greatly reduced visibility at times in blowing snow.  Keweenaw County Schools were closed on the 22nd and 23rd due to impacts from the heavy snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A west to east band of snow dropped south across northern and central Berrien County the morning of February 13th.  After a brief lull, a dominant band of lake effect snow brought additional heavy snow during the afternoon hours. Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 10 inches with localized amounts around one foot. Numerous slide-offs were reported including travel impacts to Interstate 94.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy lake effect snow with snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour dropped across the central and northern portions of Berrien County during the morning hours of February.  After a brief lull around midday, another round of heavy lake effect snow showers impacted travel again during the afternoon.  The coverage and intensity of the lake effect snow showers diminished during the evening hours. Numerous slide-offs were reported which included travel impacts to Interstate 94.  Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 10 inches with localized areas around 1 foot.  A trained spotter reported an event storm total snow accumulation of 12 inches 2 miles west of Sodus, MI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A west to east band of snow dropped south across northern and central Berrien County the morning of February 13th.  After a brief lull, a dominant band of lake effect snow brought additional heavy snow during the afternoon hours. Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 10 inches with localized amounts around one foot. Numerous slide-offs were reported including travel impacts to Interstate 94.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy lake effect snow with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour dropped across the western portions of Cass County during the morning hours of February. After a brief lull around midday, another round of moderate to heavy lake effect snow showers impacted travel again during the afternoon. The coverage and intensity of the lake effect snow showers diminished during the evening hours. Numerous slide-offs were reported. Storm total snow accumulations of 2 to 5 inches were reported with the highest amounts in the west central and northwest portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of upper disturbances lifting up from the Central Plains produced periods of moderate snow over portions of Upper Michigan while sustaining a long-duration northeast flow lake enhanced snow event into north central Upper Michigan from the 21st into the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer 48-hour lake effect snowfall totals across Alger County were between 8 and 10 inches. Northerly winds gusting to 35 mph also contributed to considerable blowing snow. Au Train-Onota Schools were closed on the 22nd and most schools in the county were closed on the 23rd due to impacts from the snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad troughing across the western half of the country provided upper-level support to deepen a surface cyclone over the Great Plains. An attendant warm front stretched from the Plains across the Great Lakes, serving as the primary forcing mechanism for a mixed-precipitation winter weather event that lead to ice accumulations of 0.25 - 0.40 across parts of central lower Michigan along and north of the boundary. The long duration of sub-freezing surface temperatures combined with above freezing temperatures aloft resulted in two rounds of freezing rain spanning 2/21 and 2/22.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous downed powerlines and tree limbs resulting in power outages across the county. Ice-covered roads were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite relatively weak upper-level support, strong mid-level warm air advection and frontogenetical forcing produced widespread snowfall across Michigan's upper peninsula from 2/5 into the morning of 2/6. South-southwest low-level flow supported lake enhanced snowfall across western Mackinac county where snowfall totals of 8 were reported in Curtis and southwest of Newberry.

Rain changed to a period of moderate to heavy snow during the afternoon and evening hours of February 17th. Total snow accumulations ranged between 3 and 7 inches, with some light ice and sleet accumulations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a brief period of sleet and freezing rain, and then over to all snow during the afternoon hours of February 17th. The snow, moderate to heavy at times, continued into the evening hours. Heavy snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour reduced visibilities to less than a quarter of a mile at times which led to difficult travel conditions. Snowfall totals across the county generally ranged between 4 and 7 inches. Light ice and sleet accumulations were also achieved before the switch over to all snow occurred. There were reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and damaging northerly winds, moderate lake effect snow and blowing snow were reported over northern Upper Michigan from the 11th into the 12th in the wake of a low pressure system tracking through the Upper Great Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer in Ironwood measured nearly eight inches of lake effect snow in 24 hours.  The snow and northerly winds gusting at or above 25 mph caused visibility in Ironwood to lower near one-half mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and damaging northerly winds, moderate lake effect snow and blowing snow were reported over northern Upper Michigan from the 11th into the 12th in the wake of a low pressure system tracking through the Upper Great Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer ten miles south of Grand Marais measured six inches of lake effect snow in 24 hours.  Northwest winds gusting over 40 mph also caused considerable blowing snow and lowered visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and damaging northerly winds, moderate lake effect snow and blowing snow were reported over northern Upper Michigan from the 11th into the 12th in the wake of a low pressure system tracking through the Upper Great Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of lake effect snow fell over the higher terrain of Marquette County over a 24-hour period.  The snow in combination with north winds gusting over 40 mph caused near whiteout conditions at times along Highway M-553 from K.I. Sawyer to Gwinn on the evening of the 11th.  Law enforcement reported minor accidents near K.I. Sawyer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and damaging northerly winds, moderate lake effect snow and blowing snow were reported over northern Upper Michigan from the 11th into the 12th in the wake of a low pressure system tracking through the Upper Great Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer in Bergland measured 6.4 inches of lake effect snow in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of upper disturbances lifting up from the Central Plains produced periods of moderate snow over portions of Upper Michigan while sustaining a long-duration northeast flow lake enhanced snow event into north central Upper Michigan from the 21st into the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant storm total lake enhanced snowfall amounts over a nearly two-day period included nearly 45 inches over the Dead River Basin, 37 inches at NWS Marquette, 30 inches in Ishpeming, over 24 inches in Big Bay and Sands Township, 22 inches at Trowbridge Park, 14 to 18 inches in the city of Marquette and 18 inches at Harvey.  Schools were closed throughout the county, including Northern Michigan University, on both the 22nd and 23rd due to the impacts from the heavy snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snowfall total over a 24-hour period included 6.8 inches at Painesdale and 5.0 inches at Kearsarge.  North winds gusting over 40 mph at times also caused occasional whiteout conditions in lake effect snow and blowing snow across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Clipper low pressure system moving across the area brought moderate snow to portions of west and central Upper Michigan from the 5th into the morning of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a public report of 7.5 inches of snow near Newberry over approximately nine hours.  Four inches of snow was reported at Helmer and McMillan during the same time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate lake effect snow downwind of Lake Superior was reported across west and north central portions of Upper Michigan from the 6th into the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Laurium measured 10.7 inches of lake effect snow over a 24-hour period. Otherwise, other observers over the higher terrain of northern Houghton County measured between 6 to 9 inches of lake effect snow in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought 6 to 14 inches of snow to southern Michigan and a daily record snowfall of 13.3 inches was set for the date in Lansing.  This resulted in numerous event cancellations and delays, school closings and traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to twelve inches of snow was reported across most of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system tracking through the Mackinac Straits produced moderate to heavy lake effect snow over portions of west and north central Upper Michigan from the evening of the 4th into the morning of the 7th.  The heaviest snow fell on the 5th for most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media and spotter lake effect snowfall totals out of the Ironwood area measured between 21 and 28 inches.  Schools were closed throughout the county on the 5th due to impacts from the heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system tracking through the Mackinac Straits produced moderate to heavy lake effect snow over portions of west and north central Upper Michigan from the evening of the 4th into the morning of the 7th.  The heaviest snow fell on the 5th for most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total lake effect snowfall reports over approximately 60 hours included 19 inches six miles north of Greenland, 16 inches in the Ontonagon area, nearly 14 inches at  Porcupine Mountains State Park and 12 inches at Bergland Dam. Ontonagon schools were closed on the 5th and delayed two hours on the 6th due to impacts from the heavy lake effect snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking across the Upper Great Lakes brought moderate snow to portions of west and central Upper Michigan on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers from Bark River through Gladstone and Escanaba to Rapid River measured between 4.0 and 4.5 inches of snow over a 9-hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking across the Upper Great Lakes brought moderate snow to portions of west and central Upper Michigan on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Painesdale measured 9.5 inches of snow in 24 hours while 5.0 inches fell at Twin Lakes and 4.8 inches of snow fell in Houghton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass moving over the region generated bitter cold chills of 25 to 35 below zero across much of west and central Upper Michigan on the morning of the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some locations over western Alger County recorded wind chill readings in the 25 to 30 below zero range on the morning of the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass enveloping the area resulted in widespread wind chills values of 25 to 30 below zero on the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dipped near 30 below zero across much of the county on the morning of the 26th.  Schools throughout the county were closed on the 26th due to bitter cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Highway M-28 was closed during much of the day and evening of the 10th from Kawbawgam Road in Harvey to Munising due to whiteout conditions in lake effect snow and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway M-28 was closed during much of the day and evening of the 10th from Kawbawgam Road in Harvey to Munising due to whiteout conditions in lake effect snow and blowing snow. The whiteout conditions resulted in the closure of Munising Schools on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of arctic air across the Upper Great Lakes along with moderate lake effect snow and considerable blowing snow from gusty northwest winds caused school closings or delays across much of western Upper Michigan on the 10th.

A potent low pressure system moving across the Upper Great Lakes produced west winds gusts over 60 mph at the Houghton County Airport on the afternoon of the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houghton County Airport ASOS measured high wind criteria west wind gusts throughout much of the period with a peak gust to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Creighton Swamp Fire burned 203 acres over a four-day period in a swamp north of M-28 in northern Schoolcraft.  The fire burned in a remote area which was difficult to access, east of County Road 454.  A lightning strike most likely caused the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 30 firefighters from the Michigan DNR and the Seney National Wildlife Refuge  fought and ultimately contained the 203-acre Creighton Swamp Wildfire over a four-day period which burned in a swamp north of Highway M-28 in northern Schoolcraft County. The fire burned in a remote area which was difficult to access, east of County Road 454.  No wheeled vehicles could access the fire so drones with infrared cameras were utilized to help delineate the full extent of the fire. DNR wetland bully engines from Gwinn and Newberry and a tractor plow from Shingleton were also utilized fighting the fire.  A lightning strike most likely caused the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple thunderstorms tracked across northern Michigan during the afternoon, including a supercell thunderstorm that produced the EF-3 tornado that hit the town of Gaylord along with very large hail in other parts of the area. ||Setting the stage for severe weather was a trough that rotated across the upper Midwest that developed and strengthened surface low pressure west of Lake Michigan. This system helped draw a warm, moist airmass northward across the state that helped provide the instability necessary to support severe thunderstorms later in the day. Storms initially formed along the cold front across Wisconsin during the morning hours and moved northeast across Lake Michigan, making it into the forecast area by early afternoon. The strongest line segment generated a measured wind gust of 76 mph at Frankfort Light and continued to produce damaging wind gusts across Leelanau and Antrim counties as it quickly moved northeast.||As this segment moved further away from the cold front, it began to transition into a supercell thunderstorm. This storm moved east-northeast across a very favorable environment in place across northern lower Michigan, eventually producing a tornado that caused considerable damage in the city of Gaylord. This supercell continued to trek across the area, producing baseball-sized hail in Posen.||For additional context, this was the first time in reliable recorded history (since 1950) that Gaylord was hit by a tornado. This was also the first significant tornado (EF2 or greater) in the county warning area since October 18, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Facebook photo of 1.5 inch hail at Tomahawk Creek Flooding. Time estimated on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado formed 4 miles east of Alba in a location on Olds Rd 0.5 miles east of the Alba Rd intersection. It tracked east northeast for about 1.75 miles before exiting Antrim county 200 yards southeast of the Alba Rd/Old Alba Rd intersection. The tornado continued in Otsego county for almost 16 additional miles before dissipating at 1457 EST. In Antrim county, no injuries or fatalities were reported. Several homes were damaged along with numerous softwood trees snapped at their trunks (DI 28, DOD 4) along Patterson Rd. While the highest rating of EF1 in Antrim county was assigned at this location, the tornado was rated EF3 in Otsego county. The maximum path width in Antrim county was 200 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper disturbance moving through a warm, moist and unstable environment set the stage for the development of isolated severe thunderstorms, heavy rainfall and flooding over portions of west and central Upper Michigan during the afternoon and evening of the 12th. Governor Whitmer declared a state of emergency for the flood damage in western Marquette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of three to four inches from thunderstorms over an approximate three-hour time period caused flooding from downtown Ishpeming across County Road 510 at the Noquemanon 510 Trailhead to County Road 550 near Wetmore Landing and Harlow Lake.  The flooding in Ishpeming caused multiple cars to get stranded in nearly 2 feet of water near the U.S. National Ski Hall of Fame and water over the road on Hill Street.  Flooding also closed portions of County Road 510 near the Noquemanon 510 Trailhead and portions of County Road 550 were closed near Wetmore Landing and Harlow Lake.  In July, Governor Whitmer declared a state of emergency for the flood damage in western Marquette County on 5/12.

This system helped draw a warm, moist airmass northward across the state that helped provide the instability necessary to support severe thunderstorms later in the day. Storms initially formed along the cold front across Wisconsin during the morning hours and moved northeast across Lake Michigan, making it into the forecast area by early afternoon. The strongest line segment generated a measured wind gust of 76 mph at Frankfort Light and continued to produce damaging wind gusts across Leelanau and Antrim counties as it quickly moved northeast.||As this segment moved further away from the cold front, it began to transition into a supercell thunderstorm. This storm moved east-northeast across a very favorable environment in place across northern lower Michigan, eventually producing a tornado that caused considerable damage in the city of Gaylord. This supercell continued to trek across the area, producing baseball-sized hail in Posen.||For additional context, this was the first time in reliable recorded history (since 1950) that Gaylord was hit by a tornado. This was also the first significant tornado (EF2 or greater) in the county warning area since October 18, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of hail smaller than baseballs resulting in roof, siding, and garage door damage to two houses.

This supercell continued to trek across the area, producing baseball-sized hail in Posen.||For additional context, this was the first time in reliable recorded history (since 1950) that Gaylord was hit by a tornado. EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado formed in Antrim county 4 miles east of Alba and continued into Otsego county 100 yards SE of the Alba Rd/Old Alba Rd intersection. The tornado continued to move east northeast for almost 16 additional miles before dissipating around 1.75 miles northeast of Sparr. The tornado was rated EF3 in Otsego county based on damage surveyed in multiple locations around the Gaylord area. The first of these locations was damage in the Nottingham Forest manufactured home community. Multiple units were completely destroyed with debris blown away from the original locations of the units (DI 4, DOD 12). The second location was the business district of Gaylord where the buildings of Hobby Lobby, Maurices, and Jimmy Johns experienced collapses of exterior walls where interior walls remained standing (DI 10, DOD 8). The last location, which had the highest estimated wind speed of 150 mph, was damage found to homes on Berkshire Ln on the northeast side of Gaylord. Most exterior walls were collapsed and only partial interior walls were left standing (DI 2, DOD 8).  The tornado dissipated at 1457 EST about 1.75 miles northeast of Sparr. Two fatalities were reported in the Nottingham Forest community, along with 44 injuries reported from various locations around Gaylord. The maximum path width in Otsego county was 200 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm with blizzard conditions impacted much of Lower Michigan just before the Christmas holiday weekend. Conditions deteriorated rapidly on the 23rd near Lake Michigan overnight and further inland during the daytime as snow moved in. The worst conditions mainly occurred between 3 PM and 6 PM on the 23rd when the wind was strongest and visibility was lowest in blowing and drifting snow. Frequent wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph (a few gusts to 55 mph) were recorded on December 23 with quarter mile visibility or less especially in West Michigan downwind of Lake Michigan, and temperatures were in the single digits to teens.  The heaviest snow fell across portions of Kent, Ottawa and Allegan counties where some areas received one to two feet of snow with 3 to 4 foot drifts.  Accidents and multi-car pile ups led to closures of some interstates and highways on Friday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot to foot and a half of snow fell across most of Ottawa county with higher amounts to near two feet over southeast portions of the county.  This combined with very strong winds which gusted to around 50 to 55 mph resulted in blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warmer, moist air moving across a shallow layer of cold air at the surface produced fairly widespread freezing rain for portions of west and central Upper Michigan from the 22nd into the 23rd.  The freezing rain resulted in treacherous and icy road conditions, tree and power line damage and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing rain of 0.25 to .40 inches caused treacherous and icy road conditions, tree damage and widespread power outages during the period especially over western and southern portions of Marquette County. Schools were cancelled on the 23rd due to the impacts from the freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds gusting over 35 mph at times in the wake of a cold front created whiteout conditions over Alger County in lake effect snow and considerable blowing snow on the 27th. The whiteout conditions caused numerous vehicles to go into the ditch along Highway M-28.  Highway M-28 was closed from Kawbawgum Road in Harvey to Munising from late morning on the 27th to early morning on the 28th due to the whiteout conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Michigan State Police reported whiteout conditions from lake effect snow and blowing snow along Highway M-28 from just east of Harvey to Munising, and as a result, the highway was closed into early morning on the 28th.  Numerous vehicles were reported in the ditch with peak northwest wind gusts exceeding 35 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system lifting north through Ontario brought a strong low-level jet max across Upper Michigan resulting in widespread damaging winds and sporadic power outages over much of west and central Upper Michigan on the 9th.  The strong winds and resulting downed power lines and relatively dry antecedent conditions also initiated several small wildfires across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were observed down in the Three Lakes area and widespread power outages were reported throughout Baraga County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported that strong south winds downed trees near the intersection of Van Buskirk and Landrud Roads southeast of Ironwood.  Power lines were also reported down.  A small brush fire was reportedly started, but put out at the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracking across northern Michigan dragged a powerful cold front through southeast Michigan during the morning hours of March 6th. Wind gusts of 45-55 mph were common behind the front, with the highest wind gusts of 60+ mph reported in Tri-Cities region and Huron county. Officially, MBS (Saginaw/Tri-Cities airport) reported a 67 mph gust as the front passed through.

Strong low pressure tracking through northern Michigan lead to strong winds of 40-60 mph over Southeast Michigan during the morning hours of March 31th. The highest winds of 60+ mph were reported across Tri-cities region, Huron county, and Flint vicinity. Power outages across southern Lower Michigan remained just under 100,000 customers.

Strong low pressure tracking through northern Michigan lead to strong winds of 40-60 mph over Southeast Michigan during the morning hours of March 31th. The highest winds of 60+ mph were reported across Tri-cities region, Huron county, and Flint vicinity. Power outages across southern Lower Michigan remained just under 100,000 customers.

A low pressure system tracking from the Central Plains into the Upper Great Lakes brought a couple of episodes of significant freezing rain and ice accumulation to portions of Upper Michigan from the 5th into the morning of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One-quarter to nearly one-half an inch of freezing rain was reported across the county over a 24-hour period which contributed to very slippery roadways and sagging tree branches. A strengthening cyclone slid across the Midwest 3/29 through 3/30, deepening to 980mb as it was over Michigan. Forcing generated by this system resulted in multiple rounds of rain and freezing rain across northern Michigan, leading to ice accumulations of 0.25 or more across various parts of the area by the afternoon of 3/30. The highest ice accumulations were seen across central Chippewa county where the KANJ ASOS reported 0.37.

A strong shortwave moving across the Midwest provided support for a cyclone that tracked from the Great Plains through the upper Great Lakes, deepening near 990mb as it slid overhead. This system produced heavy rainfall across the northern lower peninsula and a mix of winter precipitation across the eastern upper peninsula. Over 0.25 of ice accumulation came from freezing rain across Chippewa county during the evening of 3/5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm with blizzard conditions impacted much of Lower Michigan just before the Christmas holiday weekend. Conditions deteriorated rapidly on the 23rd near Lake Michigan overnight and further inland during the daytime as snow moved in. The worst conditions mainly occurred between 3 PM and 6 PM on the 23rd when the wind was strongest and visibility was lowest in blowing and drifting snow. Frequent wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph (a few gusts to 55 mph) were recorded on December 23 with quarter mile visibility or less especially in West Michigan downwind of Lake Michigan, and temperatures were in the single digits to teens.  The heaviest snow fell across portions of Kent, Ottawa and Allegan counties where some areas received one to two feet of snow with 3 to 4 foot drifts.  Accidents and multi-car pile ups led to closures of some interstates and highways on Friday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot to foot and a half of snow fell across most of Allegan county with higher amounts to near two feet over northeast Allegan county.  This combined with very strong winds which gusted to around 50 to 55 mph resulted in blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line raced across southern lower Michigan during the afternoon and evening of August 29, 2022, leaving widespread wind damage in its path. This line of storms developed ahead of a cold front and tracked into the region from the Chicago area, expanding in coverage and intensity as they encountered a warm, muggy, and unstable atmosphere over Southeast Michigan. Gusts of 60 to 75 mph were responsible for damage to trees, power lines, and property and left over 375,000 customers without power. Several gustnados were reported along the leading edge of the strong gust front in Cambridge Twp, Highland Twp, Putnam/Hamburg Twp, Richmond, and Fort Gratiot, producing localized corridors of enhanced damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch fell onto a pickup truck cab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of thunderstorms brought widespread strong to severe thunderstorms across southeast Michigan. Thunderstorms initially became organized across the Tri-Cities and northern Thumb during the early afternoon and brought widely scattered wind gusts between 50-60 mph, which resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Severe thunderstorms continued to expand southward during the late afternoon, while new storms developed just ahead of the initial line, along an expanding outflow boundary. Given the moderate to strong instability in place, thunderstorms continued to produce widely scattered damaging wind gusts as they approached the northern Metro region. A Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) eventually caught up with the line in the evening hours which continued the threat of severe weather across the Detroit Metro region. Over 100,000 homes and businesses were without power after the thunderstorms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a Dodge Ram truck traveling on the road. The tree went through the trucks windshield and entered the passenger compartment, killing a 36 year old woman passenger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 46-acre wildfire burned over a three-day period approximately three-quarters of a mile south of the 12 Mile Beach Campground or just south of the Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore in Alger County.  The Michigan DNR, Burt Township Fire Department and the National Park Service crews responded to the fire and worked throughout the night to get the fire successfully contained and to ensure the safety of campers and park visitors.  DNR crews manned a tractor plow and nine engines while fighting the fire.  A cause of the wildfire was not immediately determined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 46-acre wildfire burned over a three-day period approximately three-quarters of a mile south of the 12 Mile Beach Campground or just south of the Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore in Alger County. The Michigan DNR, Burt Township Fire Department and the National Park Service crews responded to the fire and worked throughout the night to get the fire successfully contained and to ensure the safety of campers and park visitors. DNR crews manned a tractor plow and nine engines while fighting the fire. A cause of the wildfire was not immediately determined.

A Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) eventually caught up with the line in the evening hours which continued the threat of severe weather across the Detroit Metro region. Over 100,000 homes and businesses were without power after the thunderstorms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 2 foot diameter tree was sheared off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line raced across southern lower Michigan during the afternoon and evening of August 29, 2022, leaving widespread wind damage in its path. This line of storms developed ahead of a cold front and tracked into the region from the Chicago area, expanding in coverage and intensity as they encountered a warm, muggy, and unstable atmosphere over Southeast Michigan. Gusts of 60 to 75 mph were responsible for damage to trees, power lines, and property and left over 375,000 customers without power. Several gustnados were reported along the leading edge of the strong gust front in Cambridge Twp, Highland Twp, Putnam/Hamburg Twp, Richmond, and Fort Gratiot, producing localized corridors of enhanced damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees downed across Richmond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy lake effect snow affected southwest Michigan late afternoon on November 19th through the early morning hours of November 20th. Intense snowfall rates with near zero visibility created difficult travel conditions. Snowfall accumulations generally ranged between 2 and 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers, temperatures falling into the teens, and gusty winds to 40 mph developed behind an arctic cold front late on November 19th into the early morning hours of November 20th. Reduced visibilities and brief intense snowfall rates were observed with more intense lake effect bands, especially during the evening hours on November 19th. Blowing snow and snow covered roads occurred as a result with difficult travel and a few accidents reported across the region. Snow accumulations were estimated to be in the 1 to 3 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of thunderstorms brought widespread strong to severe thunderstorms across southeast Michigan. Thunderstorms initially became organized across the Tri-Cities and northern Thumb during the early afternoon and brought widely scattered wind gusts between 50-60 mph, which resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Severe thunderstorms continued to expand southward during the late afternoon, while new storms developed just ahead of the initial line, along an expanding outflow boundary. Given the moderate to strong instability in place, thunderstorms continued to produce widely scattered damaging wind gusts as they approached the northern Metro region. A Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) eventually caught up with the line in the evening hours which continued the threat of severe weather across the Detroit Metro region. Over 100,000 homes and businesses were without power after the thunderstorms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy lake effect snow affected southwest Michigan late afternoon on November 19th through the early morning hours of November 20th. Intense snowfall rates with near zero visibility created difficult travel conditions. Snowfall accumulations generally ranged between 2 and 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow showers, temperatures falling into the teens, and gusty winds to 40 mph developed behind an arctic cold front late on November 19th into the early morning hours of November 20th. Near zero visibilities and intense 1 per hour snowfall rates were observed with more intense lake effect bands, especially during the evening hours on November 19th. Blowing snow and snow covered roads occurred as a result with difficult travel and accidents reports across the region. Snow accumulations were estimated to be in the 3 to 8 inch range, heaviest near the Indiana border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line raced across southern lower Michigan during the afternoon and evening of August 29, 2022, leaving widespread wind damage in its path. This line of storms developed ahead of a cold front and tracked into the region from the Chicago area, expanding in coverage and intensity as they encountered a warm, muggy, and unstable atmosphere over Southeast Michigan. Gusts of 60 to 75 mph were responsible for damage to trees, power lines, and property and left over 375,000 customers without power. Several gustnados were reported along the leading edge of the strong gust front in Cambridge Twp, Highland Twp, Putnam/Hamburg Twp, Richmond, and Fort Gratiot, producing localized corridors of enhanced damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at the National Weather Service office in White Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm with blizzard conditions impacted much of Lower Michigan just before the Christmas holiday weekend. Conditions deteriorated rapidly on the 23rd near Lake Michigan overnight and further inland during the daytime as snow moved in. The worst conditions mainly occurred between 3 PM and 6 PM on the 23rd when the wind was strongest and visibility was lowest in blowing and drifting snow. Frequent wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph (a few gusts to 55 mph) were recorded on December 23 with quarter mile visibility or less especially in West Michigan downwind of Lake Michigan, and temperatures were in the single digits to teens.  The heaviest snow fell across portions of Kent, Ottawa and Allegan counties where some areas received one to two feet of snow with 3 to 4 foot drifts.  Accidents and multi-car pile ups led to closures of some interstates and highways on Friday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions occurred due to eight to sixteen inches of snow across Van Buren county which combined with very strong winds which gusted to around 50 to 55 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy, wet lake effect snow developed during the early morning hours of November 16th, and persisted through mid-morning on November 17th. Snowfall totals between 6 and 10 inches were common, with localized amounts in excess of a foot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy lake effect snow accumulated to between 3 and 8 inches, heaviest inland near Paw Paw Lake. The heavy, wet nature to the snow led to some downed tree limbs and brief power outages. Roads become slick and snow covered in heavier snow bands, with reports of accidents and slide-offs in the area. School delays and closings were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a few late evening severe thunderstorms between the I-69 and M-59 corridors. Damaging winds and a weak tornado near Lake Fenton occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey confirmed a weak tornado developed over or along the shoreline of eastern Lake Fenton during the late evening of July 11th. The tornado tracked east-southeast for 7.5 miles before lifting 3 miles east-southeast of Holly/just west of I-75 in northern Oakland County. The first indication of damage occurred on Pine Street near Lake Fenton where large limbs fell on a home, which took out a second-story dormer and a portion of the roof. Tree damage was sparse and sporadic along the path, mainly consisting of tree limbs and leaves down. The weak tornado crossed into northwest Oakland County just southwest of Oyster Lake, and continued tracking southeast for an additional 6.1 miles. The peak damage was observed in the backyard of a home on Addis Road in Holly. About a dozen trees suffered significant damage. The home also lost a few vinyl siding panels and a pergola was damaged. A barn farther down the road lost some metal roofing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several upper level disturbances and an arctic air outbreak resulted in a prolonged period of heavy lake effect effect and synoptic snow.  The most prolific snow was reported in Gobles in Van Buren county where 28 inches was reported.  Grand Rapids was also hard hit with several locations in and near the city reporting 24 to 27 inches of snow from the 17th through the 20th.  Areas from Kalamazoo north to Grand Rapids and off to the west were the hardest hit by heavy lake effect snow.  The combined effects of snow and falling temperatures resulted in snow covered to icy road conditions and numerous accidents and school closing and event cancellations across southwestern lower Michigan from the 17th-20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around six to eight inches of snow fell across portions of Osceola county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: North northwest winds coming across relatively mild Lake Michigan waters resulted in locally heavy lake effect snow.  Up to ten inches of snow was reported in Freesoil in Mason county.  There were additional reports of snow in the seven to eight inch range across portions of Van Buren county. This resulted in hazardous travel conditions in those areas that received this locally heavy lake effect snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of lake effect snow was reported in Freesoil.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass send heat indices soaring from 95 to 102 degrees across much of west and central Upper Michigan on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices peaked at 102 degrees near Peavy Falls Dam during the early afternoon of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass send heat indices soaring from 95 to 102 degrees across much of west and central Upper Michigan on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices across Marquette County peaked between 95 and 100 degrees during the early to mid-afternoon hours of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several upper level disturbances and an arctic air outbreak resulted in a prolonged period of heavy lake effect effect and synoptic snow.  The most prolific snow was reported in Gobles in Van Buren county where 28 inches was reported.  Grand Rapids was also hard hit with several locations in and near the city reporting 24 to 27 inches of snow from the 17th through the 20th.  Areas from Kalamazoo north to Grand Rapids and off to the west were the hardest hit by heavy lake effect snow.  The combined effects of snow and falling temperatures resulted in snow covered to icy road conditions and numerous accidents and school closing and event cancellations across southwestern lower Michigan from the 17th-20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around six to eight inches of snow was reported across portions of Lake county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive trough progressed over the Great Lakes 11/30, providing strong ascent aloft to support a sub-980mb cyclone near Hudson Bay. Strong westerly winds gusting from 30-50 mph across much of northern Michigan combined with lake effect snow to create rapid reductions in visibility and hazardous driving conditions. Presque Isle county was most impacted by these conditions, measuring 6 inches of snow in about a 10 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers intensified on the evening of November 12th and continued through the early morning hours of November 13th. The heaviest snow accumulations occurred across portions of Berrien County where 5 to 9 inches of snowfall were received. Impacts from the snowfall increased during the early morning hours on November 13th as temperatures dropped below freezing allowing for snow-covered roads to develop and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell during the evening hours of November 12th extending through much of the early morning hours of November 13th. Total snowfall ranged from 5 to 9 inches. A public report of 8 inches of snow accumulation was received at 7 AM LST November 13th in Benton Harbor, MI.  The snow was heavy and wet in nature and started to accumulate on roadways late in the evening as temperatures dropped below freezing. Roads became slick with some slide-offs reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell during the evening hours of November 12th extending through much of the early morning hours of November 13th. Total snowfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches. A public report of 6 inches of snow accumulation was received at 11:11 AM LST November 13th in Berrien Springs, MI.  The snow was heavy and wet in nature and started to accumulate on roadways late in the evening as temperatures dropped below freezing. Roads became slick with some slide-offs reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of lake effect snow, blowing snow, reduced visibilities and gusty winds developed late November 17th and continued through the day on November 18th bringing difficult travel conditions. Snow accumulation of 2 to 6 inches were common across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another round of lake effect snow accumulated to between 2 and 6 inches, heaviest during the morning hours of November 18th when snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were briefly reported. Temperatures falling into the 20s, snow already on the ground, and gusty winds also allowed for areas of blowing snow and reduced visibilities. Travel was difficult across the area with reports of slide-offs, accidents and school closings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying line of thunderstorms exited Wisconsin and provided a glancing blow to Michigan and Indiana. There was least one instance of tree damage in northern Indiana and sub severe hail in Michigan. In the vicinity of this line of storms, wind was strong with gusts near 50mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle size hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong thunderstorms formed along an advancing cold front early in the morning on 9/3, eventually tracking into northern lower Michigan and producing heavy rainfall. Additional storms initiated just behind the line and continued to produce heavy rainfall in the vicinity of Little Traverse Bay, leading to significant erosion of the shoulder of M-119. A 24 hour rainfall total of 2.80 inches was measured 1 mile NNE of Harbor Springs at 9:30 AM EST with the majority of that falling in a 3 hour period that morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: M-119 (Bluff Dr) closed at Harrison St due to significant erosion of shoulder of highway. Time estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Expansive troughing settled over the Great Lakes for several days, providing varied times of favorable forcing aloft as embedded shortwaves pivoted through the main flow. Weak winds, cold air temperatures, and relatively warm lake surface temperatures also combined to create a favorable environment for mesoscale lows to develop over the open waters. Multiple lows developed over the course of 11/17 and 11/18, the most impressive one dropping 24 inches of snow across portions of Charlevoix and Antrim county over a 12 hour period overnight. Areas across Emmet and Cheboygan counties measured 16 inches of snow with other counties also measuring around 10 inches or more.

Longwave troughing encompassing much of the northeast CONUS continued to pivot over the region for several days, providing support for a weak area of low pressure to slide across southern Ontario. This system provided southwesterly lake enhanced snowfall across the Tip of the Mitt and the far eastern upper peninsula 11/18 into 11/19. Low-level winds then shifted more westerly/west-northwesterly later in the day 11/19, refocusing lake effect snowfall to the typical lake effect snowbelts. Snowfall amounts in the 8-10 inch range was measured across much of northwest lower and eastern upper Michigan.

A major winter storm with blizzard conditions impacted much of Lower Michigan just before the Christmas holiday weekend. Conditions deteriorated rapidly on the 23rd near Lake Michigan overnight and further inland during the daytime as snow moved in. The worst conditions mainly occurred between 3 PM and 6 PM on the 23rd when the wind was strongest and visibility was lowest in blowing and drifting snow. Frequent wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph (a few gusts to 55 mph) were recorded on December 23 with quarter mile visibility or less especially in West Michigan downwind of Lake Michigan, and temperatures were in the single digits to teens.  The heaviest snow fell across portions of Kent, Ottawa and Allegan counties where some areas received one to two feet of snow with 3 to 4 foot drifts.  Accidents and multi-car pile ups led to closures of some interstates and highways on Friday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half a foot to foot of snow fell across Barry county.  This combined with very strong winds which gusted to around 50 mph resulted in blizzard conditions.

Frequent wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph (a few gusts to 55 mph) were recorded on December 23 with quarter mile visibility or less especially in West Michigan downwind of Lake Michigan, and temperatures were in the single digits to teens.  The heaviest snow fell across portions of Kent, Ottawa and Allegan counties where some areas received one to two feet of snow with 3 to 4 foot drifts.  Accidents and multi-car pile ups led to closures of some interstates and highways on Friday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to eight inches of snow was reported along with strong winds that resulted in near whiteout conditions as times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front associated with a winter storm brought dangerous wind chills to area from the early morning hours of December 23rd to the early morning hours of December 24th. The combination of temperatures in the single digits above zero to single digits below zero and wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph resulted in dangerous wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic front brought sub-zero temperatures and strong wind gusts to 40 to 50 mph. This combination resulted in dangerous wind chills of 25 to 35 below zero from the early morning hours on December 23rd to the early morning hours on December 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly deepening and large low pressure system moving through the area produced accumulating snow, strong winds, and bitter cold temperatures. Snowfall accumulations mostly ranged from 2 to 6 inches across southeast Michigan, with the highest amounts occurring along and around the I-69 corridor where isolated higher amounts around 8 inches were reported. Prolonged strong winds with gusts of 40-55 MPH caused visibilities to frequently drop below 1 mile in snow and blowing snow, leading to snow covered and hazardous road conditions. Brief white out conditions were even reported across the Thumb region. Temperatures falling into the single numbers lead to wind chills bottoming out around -20 F.|Here are some of the higher multi-day snowfall totals received:||Owosso...                       8.5 inches.|Lapeer....                        8.3 inches.|Flint Bishop Airport...       6.3 inches.|Howell...                         5.9 inches.|Auburn...                        5.3 inches.|Ann Arbor...                    5.2 inches.|Detroit Metro Airport...     4.6 inches.|White Lake...                   4.1 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving through the Upper Great Lakes brought periods of freezing rain, sleet and snow to Upper Michigan from the 14th into the 15th.  Moderate to heavy snow fell on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer in Ironwood measured six inches of wet, heavy snow in approximately five hours. Schools were closed throughout much of Gogebic County on the 15th due to impacts from the heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate lake effect snow fell over portions of Marquette and Alger counties from the evening of the 17th into the morning of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snow and poor road conditions contributed to a fatal two-vehicle crash near McMillan in Lakefield Township on the morning of the 18th.  At 9:50 am MSP troopers responded to a two-vehicle crash near the intersection of Highway M-28 and County Road 444 in Lakefield Township.  A 19-year-old man from Newberry was driving east on M-28 when his Ford F-250 truck crossed the center line and collided with a Chrysler minivan driven by a 64-year-old woman from Newberry.  The woman was pronounced dead at the scene of the crash.  The man was taken to Helen Newberry Joy Hospital for non-life-threatening injuries and released.  The MSP was assisted at the scene of the accident by the MDNR, Columbus Fire Department, the Luce County Sheriff's office and Luce County EMS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving through the Upper Great Lakes brought periods of freezing rain, sleet and snow to Upper Michigan from the 14th into the 15th.  Moderate to heavy snow fell on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer just northeast of Iron Mountain measured six inches of wet snow in approximately six hours.  The observer in Norway measured 4.4 inches of snow and reported that the power was knocked out for an hour. There were Facebook reports of an estimated eight inches of snow in Sagola and five inches of snow in Kingsford over the same time period. Some freezing rain fell for several hours before the onset of the snow.  Schools were closed throughout Dickinson County on the 15th due to impacts from the heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving through the Upper Great Lakes brought periods of freezing rain, sleet and snow to Upper Michigan from the 14th into the 15th.  Moderate to heavy snow fell on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Marquette in Negaunee Township measured over six inches of wet snow in approximately five hours and a NWS employee near National Mine measured 6.7 inches over the same period.  The heavy intense snow caused very hazardous travel in the Marquette area for the morning commute and schools were closed throughout Marquette County on the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving through the Upper Great Lakes brought periods of freezing rain, sleet and snow to Upper Michigan from the 14th into the 15th.  Moderate to heavy snow fell on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread four to five inches of wet snow was observed over an approximate five-hour period. Schools were closed throughout Baraga County on the 15th due to the impacts from the heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate lake effect snow fell over portions of Marquette and Alger counties from the evening of the 17th into the morning of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media report of 5.5 inches of lake effect snow in Harvey over less than three hours.  Much of the snow reportedly fell over a two-hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate lake effect snow fell over portions of Marquette and Alger counties from the evening of the 17th into the morning of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snowfall totals over less than a 12-hour period included 8.5 inches at Munising, 5.5 inches at Deerton and 4.7 inches near Forest Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly deepening storm system over the Upper Great Lakes produced a long-duration lake enhanced snow event across Upper Michigan from the 21st into the morning of the 26th. Blizzard conditions from lake enhanced snow and blowing snow were also reported across much of the area from the 23rd into the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three-day lake enhanced snowfall totals from observers in Baraga County included 42.8 inches two miles south of Baraga, 35.3 inches at Herman and nearly 30 inches at Covington. Northwest winds gusting over 30 mph at times also caused considerable blowing of snow and occasional whiteout conditions from the 24th into the 25th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marquette National Weather Service in Negaunee Township and the observer near Big Bay reported three-day totals of more than 24 inches of lake enhanced snowfall ending in the early morning of the 25th. Blizzard conditions were reported in the Marquette area from late on the 23rd through the 24th as northwest winds gusted at or above 40 mph at times.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multi-day lake enhanced storm snowfall totals included 32 inches at Halfway Lake and over 21 inches at the Luce County Road Commission in Newberry.  Northwest winds gusting to 35 mph at times caused blizzard conditions in snow and blowing snow, especially on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly deepening and large low pressure system moving through the area produced accumulating snow, strong winds, and bitter cold temperatures. Snowfall accumulations mostly ranged from 2 to 6 inches across southeast Michigan, with the highest amounts occurring along and around the I-69 corridor where isolated higher amounts around 8 inches were reported. Prolonged strong winds with gusts of 40-55 MPH caused visibilities to frequently drop below 1 mile in snow and blowing snow, leading to snow covered and hazardous road conditions. Brief white out conditions were even reported across the Thumb region. Temperatures falling into the single numbers lead to wind chills bottoming out around -20 F.|Here are some of the higher multi-day snowfall totals received:||Owosso...                       8.5 inches.|Lapeer....                        8.3 inches.|Flint Bishop Airport...       6.3 inches.|Howell...                         5.9 inches.|Auburn...                        5.3 inches.|Ann Arbor...                    5.2 inches.|Detroit Metro Airport...     4.6 inches.|White Lake...                   4.1 inches.

A major winter storm with blizzard conditions impacted much of Lower Michigan just before the Christmas holiday weekend. Conditions deteriorated rapidly on the 23rd near Lake Michigan overnight and further inland during the daytime as snow moved in. The worst conditions mainly occurred between 3 PM and 6 PM on the 23rd when the wind was strongest and visibility was lowest in blowing and drifting snow. Frequent wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph (a few gusts to 55 mph) were recorded on December 23 with quarter mile visibility or less especially in West Michigan downwind of Lake Michigan, and temperatures were in the single digits to teens.  The heaviest snow fell across portions of Kent, Ottawa and Allegan counties where some areas received one to two feet of snow with 3 to 4 foot drifts.  Accidents and multi-car pile ups led to closures of some interstates and highways on Friday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches to a foot of snow fell across Oceana county which combined with very strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment of over 4000 J/KG of SBCAPE, combined with 45 to 60 knots of effective shear set the stage for multiple rounds of thunderstorm during the afternoon and evening hours of June 13th. Initial storms remained poorly organized, but did produce sporadic wind damage or hail. More substantial thunderstorm development evolved during the evening hours in northwestern Indiana and southwestern Lower Michigan and progressed southeast, eventually producing several swaths of wind damage. In far southern Lower Michigan, damage occurred in portions of Berrien, St Joseph and Branch counties with the most intense occurring from Three Rivers to Sturgis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Three Rivers Airport recorded a 66 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong thunderstorms initiated across far northeast lower Michigan near Saginaw Bay during the afternoon/early evening hours of 7/23 amidst weak forcing and modest instability. One storm ended up producing severe winds that resulted in trees down south of the West Branch area while the other moved out over Lake Huron quickly after forming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of trees down in northeastern Gladwin county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonably strong trough progressed from the northern Great Plains over the Midwest on 6/15, providing ample forcing aloft to support convective initiation along a cold front stretching across portions of WI, MN, and IA during the afternoon. These storms quickly grew upscale into a line that continued to travel northeast through the evening. Eventually, these storms tracked across portions of the northwest Lower Peninsula and the eastern Upper Peninsula, producing marginally severe hail and damaging wind gusts late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated brief quarter size hail. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough rotated over the northern Great Lakes on 6/28, helping to support a cluster of thunderstorms that initiated across the eastern upper peninsula of Michigan during the afternoon. These storms passed over Paradise, where they produced 0.75 hail stones just before moving over Whitefish Bay.

A cold front tracking through southeast Michigan during the afternoon hours of July 20, 2022 led to widespread thunderstorm development and severe weather reports across the area. The main threat with these storms was damaging winds, as the line of storms moved through between 1-4 PM. Several reports of trees and powerlines down as well as a 66 mph wind gust at Detroit Metro Airport were recorded. A second round of storms developed between 4-6 PM as the cold front cleared the area, primarily impacting the extreme eastern portions of the CWA near the Lake Huron shoreline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through a moist and unstable airmass spawned a line of particularly strong severe thunderstorms over portions of south central Upper Michigan on the evening of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS storm survey team observed widespread damage from Faithorn to Hermansville with several structures severely damaged and thousands of trees uprooted or snapped off.  Downed trees and powerlines resulted in impassable roads. |The NWS survey team also concluded that the damage was characteristic of intense straight line winds of 100 to 110 mph.  This is supported by radar data which showed a classic descending rear inflow jet (strong high altitude winds descending to the ground) surging northeastward across this area. In addition, the downed trees fell in mainly the same direction along with signs of divergent flow in the damage pattern.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm moved southeast across Lake Michigan from near Milwaukee and produced numerous reports of large hail up to an inch in diameter and significant wind damage in the form of hundreds of downed trees, tree limbs and numerous power lines from near South Haven southeast to Vicksburg.  Damage was significant and cleanup was still occurring for nearly two weeks after the storms struck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported numerous trees and tree limbs and power lines down across mainly southern Van Buren county.  Hail up to one inch in diameter was also reported. Two miles west of Lawrence there was a higher concentration of several dozen large healthy tree trunks snapped, uprooted, or large limbs snapped. Damage occurred to at least 3 residences due to fallen trees, one probably a total loss, another with significant roof damage. Cleanup was still ongoing 12 days after the storms struck. Just east of Hartford, hundreds of trees were scattered within approximately a 6 square-mile area with large limbs snapped, trunks snapped, or uprooted. Damage was centered in and near the Van Buren County Youth Fair. The Van Buren Historical Society Museum had damage to the back of the building due to fallen tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm moved southeast across Lake Michigan from near Milwaukee and produced numerous reports of large hail up to an inch in diameter and significant wind damage in the form of hundreds of downed trees, tree limbs and numerous power lines from near South Haven southeast to Vicksburg.  Damage was significant and cleanup was still occurring for nearly two weeks after the storms struck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 75 mph brought down numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines in Texas township and to the southeast near Schoolcraft and Vicksburg.
