EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy lake effect snow occurred from the 29th into the 30th over western Upper MI behind a deepening surface low moving across the Upper Great Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Ironwood measured 10.3 inches of lake effect snow over a 24-hour period.  Most of the snow fell in the 12-hour period ending the evening of the 29th. Lesser snow amounts were reported well inland as the observer near Watersmeet measured only four inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy lake effect snow occurred from the 29th into the 30th over western Upper MI behind a deepening surface low moving across the Upper Great Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Bergland Dam measured 10.8 inches of lake effect snow in 24 hours.  Most of the snow fell in the first 13 hours ending during the evening of the 29th. Lesser snow amounts were reported closer to Lake Superior during the event as the spotter near Ontonagon measured 5.3 inches of snow over 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air wrapping behind a low pressure system and associated cold front resulted in a long-duration moderate lake effect snow event for the north wind snow belts of Upper Michigan from the 11th through the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snowfall totals over the 24-hour period ending the morning of the 13th included 8.5 inches at Carlshend, 7.6 inches five miles southwest of National Mine, 7 inches at Clarksburg and 6 inches from Sands Township to Gwinn.  Several multicar accidents from icy roads were reported in the Marquette area on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense mesolow circulations over western Lake Superior and northern Lake Michigan brought moderate to heavy snow to portions of west and central Upper Michigan from the 17th into the 18th.  There were several reports of 3-inch per hour snowfall rates in the Ironwood area during the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers and snow spotters in and around the Ironwood area measured between 20 and 24 inches of lake effect snow in approximately a 20-hour period ending on the morning of the 18th. There were several reports of 3-inch per hour snowfall rates in the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 17th when the snow intensity was at its peak.  There was also a 24-hour report of 16.5 inches of snow in Wakefield.  Most schools were closed on the 18th due to impacts from the heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense mesolow circulations over western Lake Superior and northern Lake Michigan brought moderate to heavy snow to portions of west and central Upper Michigan from the 17th into the 18th.  There were several reports of 3-inch per hour snowfall rates in the Ironwood area during the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snowfall totals over a 24-hour period ending on the morning of the 18th included 13 inches at the observing site eight north of Mass City, 9.8 inches at Bergland Dam and 6.4 inches at Paulding.  Many schools were closed throughout Ontonagon County on the 18th due to impacts from the heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense mesolow circulations over western Lake Superior and northern Lake Michigan brought moderate to heavy snow to portions of west and central Upper Michigan from the 17th into the 18th.  There were several reports of 3-inch per hour snowfall rates in the Ironwood area during the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Twin Lakes measured 15.6 inches of lake effect snow in approximately 28 hours.  The observer at Painesdale measured 8.5 inches in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly-deepening low pressure system lifting north through Wisconsin and western Upper Michigan brought very strong south winds to portions of southeast Upper Michigan during the late afternoon of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds were observed at the Fairport GLOS during the period with a peak gust to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense mesolow circulations over western Lake Superior and northern Lake Michigan brought moderate to heavy snow to portions of west and central Upper Michigan from the 17th into the 18th.  There were several reports of 3-inch per hour snowfall rates in the Ironwood area during the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Herman measured 6 inches of lake effect snow in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense mesolow circulations over western Lake Superior and northern Lake Michigan brought moderate to heavy snow to portions of west and central Upper Michigan from the 17th into the 18th.  There were several reports of 3-inch per hour snowfall rates in the Ironwood area during the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Parent Bay measured 6 inches of lake effect snow in 24 hours from a mesolow over northern Lake Michigan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through the area brought moderate to heavy lake effect snow to the northwest wind snow belts of Upper Michigan from the 18th into the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reports over Alger County included 7.5 inches of lake effect snow in Au Train over approximately nine hours and 8.6 inches in Munising in less than 14 hours.  There was a 90-minute period ending just after midnight on the 20th where five inches of snow fell in Munising. Northwest winds gusting to 40 to 45 mph accompanying the heavy snow also caused whiteout conditions at times, especially along Lake Superior.  Several accidents were reported along M-28 between Au Train and Munising.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly-deepening low pressure system lifting north through Wisconsin and western Upper Michigan brought very strong south winds to portions of southeast Upper Michigan during the late afternoon of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer in Parent Bay measured strong south winds through the period with a peak gust of 68 mph  The observer's bird feeder was knocked down and his power was fluctuating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system with blizzard conditions impacted much of the Great Lakes heading into the Christmas Holiday. Heavy wet snow moved into Northern Michigan the evening of 12/22 with some drizzle and rain closer to Saginaw Bay and the Lake Huron shoreline. The arctic front blasted through overnight with temperatures falling into the teens and staying there through much of the weekend. As colder air moved into the region, lake effect processes kicked into gear. This led to the highest snowfall totals in the typical northwest-flow belts of northern lower and eastern upper Michigan. Storm totals through Christmas ranged from 2 to 5��� closer to Saginaw Bay to a wide swath of 1 to 2 feet in northwest Lower. Localized amounts exceeding 30 were observed in the Gaylord area with the highest storm total report across the lower peninsula coming from Mancelona at 36.7. Many areas across the eastern upper peninsula also received over 30 of snow, including Dafter (31) and Sault Ste Marie (43.1 from 12/22 - 12/26). Winds gusting into the 45 to 55mph range (with some higher gusts near the Lake Michigan shore) led to significant blowing and drifting snow with white-out conditions across most of the region. Travel was significantly impacted across most of the state through Christmas with numerous accidents, abandoned vehicles, and road closures.

A long duration winter storm affected southern Lower Michigan with multiple hazards including heavy snow, blowing snow, strong winds, whiteouts, and dangerous wind chills. Blizzard conditions were experienced across portions of northwest and north central Indiana. Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 16 inches across far southwest Lower Michigan to 2 to 4 inches across south central Lower Michigan. The storm brought very difficult to impossible travel conditions across the area. High impacts to travel were reported even across areas which experienced the lower snowfall totals. Dangerous winds chills of 20 below to 35 below zero were reported from late Friday night through late Saturday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought snow and blowing snow that led to blizzard conditions at times from early Friday morning through early Saturday morning. Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches with the highest amounts across the northern portions of the county. Widespread blowing and drifting snow led to whiteout conditions at times with wind gusts as high as 50 mph. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported.

Blizzard conditions were experienced across portions of northwest and north central Indiana. Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 16 inches across far southwest Lower Michigan to 2 to 4 inches across south central Lower Michigan. The storm brought very difficult to impossible travel conditions across the area. High impacts to travel were reported even across areas which experienced the lower snowfall totals. Dangerous winds chills of 20 below to 35 below zero were reported from late Friday night through late Saturday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought snow and blowing snow that led to blizzard conditions at times from early Friday morning through early Saturday evening. Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 14 inches. A CoCoRaHS observer reported 15.0 inches of snow accumulation 1 mile south southeast of Bertrand, MI. Widespread blowing and drifting snow led to blizzard conditions at times with wind gusts as high as 50 mph. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported which included Interstate 94. Travel became very difficult to impossible with several road closures reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system with blizzard conditions impacted much of the Great Lakes heading into the Christmas Holiday. Heavy wet snow moved into Northern Michigan the evening of 12/22 with some drizzle and rain closer to Saginaw Bay and the Lake Huron shoreline. The arctic front blasted through overnight with temperatures falling into the teens and staying there through much of the weekend. As colder air moved into the region, lake effect processes kicked into gear. This led to the highest snowfall totals in the typical northwest-flow belts of northern lower and eastern upper Michigan. Storm totals through Christmas ranged from 2 to 5��� closer to Saginaw Bay to a wide swath of 1 to 2 feet in northwest Lower. Localized amounts exceeding 30 were observed in the Gaylord area with the highest storm total report across the lower peninsula coming from Mancelona at 36.7. Many areas across the eastern upper peninsula also received over 30 of snow, including Dafter (31) and Sault Ste Marie (43.1 from 12/22 - 12/26). Winds gusting into the 45 to 55mph range (with some higher gusts near the Lake Michigan shore) led to significant blowing and drifting snow with white-out conditions across most of the region. Travel was significantly impacted across most of the state through Christmas with numerous accidents, abandoned vehicles, and road closures.
